ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (aubergine/eggplant cooked) is the closest defensible match. No specific code exists for the Baingan Patiyala composite dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Baingan Patiyala is approximately 250 g, typically served as a side dish alongside roti, naan, or rice in a North Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Brinjal (cooked) and composite spiced vegetable dish estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (brinjal cooked, spiced vegetable preparations) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (eggplant cooked, boiled, drained; FDC ID 169228) for macro validation. The input record values (energy 112 kcal, protein 2.4 g, fat 7.2 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with a stir-fried spiced brinjal preparation with added oil and spices, and are retained as the best available estimate. Micronutrient values (calcium 20 mg, iron 0.6 mg, vitamin C 8 mg) are consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA data for cooked eggplant with spices.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Baingan Patiyala. GI estimated at 15 based on the analogous food 'eggplant / aubergine, cooked' from the University of Sydney GI Database, which reports a GI of approximately 15 (low GI food). Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (15 × 25.0) / 100 = 3.75, rounded to 3.8. This is consistent with the low carbohydrate content (10 g/100 g) and high fibre (3.2 g/100 g) of the dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (180 mg/100 g) with favourable potassium content (~285 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Baingan Patiyala is suitable for hypertensive individuals given its sodium content of 180 mg/100 g (well below the 400 mg threshold) and estimated potassium of 285 mg/100 g from aubergine, tomato, and onion; however, patients should confirm no additional salt is added during home or restaurant preparation and monitor total sodium across the full meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~15), moderate carbohydrate (10 g/100 g), and high dietary fibre (3.2 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baingan Patiyala is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low GI (~15), low glycemic load per serving (~3.8), and 3.2 g/100 g dietary fibre which slows glucose absorption; it should be paired with low-GI accompaniments such as whole wheat roti or brown rice rather than white rice or naan to maintain overall meal glycemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and anti-inflammatory spice base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baingan Patiyala presents a low cardiovascular risk profile with saturated fat at only 1.0 g/100 g, no trans fat, no cholesterol, and an anti-inflammatory spice base (turmeric, ginger, garlic) that may support endothelial health; total fat at 7.2 g/100 g from cooking oil should be monitored in patients with dyslipidaemia, and oil quantity should be minimised during preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) but nightshade vegetables present (aubergine, tomato, chilli)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The turmeric (curcumin), ginger (gingerols), and garlic in Baingan Patiyala provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefits; however, RA patients with known nightshade sensitivity should monitor for symptom flares as aubergine, tomato, and green chilli are solanaceous vegetables that may aggravate inflammation in susceptible individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baingan Patiyala (बैंगन पटियाला) is a North Indian vegetable curry originating from the Punjab region, featuring tender cubes of aubergine (eggplant / brinjal) stir-fried with a fragrant base of onion, tomato, garlic, ginger, and green chilli, seasoned with turmeric, cumin, coriander powder, and garam masala, and finished with fresh coriander leaves. The dish has a soft, slightly smoky texture from the aubergine and a rich, spiced tomato-onion gravy. It is entirely plant-based — vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free — making it widely suitable across dietary requirements. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 112 kcal, 2.4 g protein, 7.2 g fat (predominantly from cooking oil, with only 1.0 g saturated fat and no trans fat or cholesterol), 10.0 g carbohydrate, 3.2 g dietary fibre, and 180 mg sodium. The glycemic index is very low (~15), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 3.8 per 250 g serving, making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The anti-inflammatory spice base (turmeric, ginger, garlic) provides additional clinical relevance for cardiovascular and inflammatory conditions. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at North Indian restaurants such as Curry Village (Boat Quay) and Bawarchi (South Bridge Road), typically as a side dish with roti, naan, or rice. A standard serving is approximately 250 g.
common_user_queries: Is baingan patiyala good for diabetics?
What is baingan patiyala made of?
Is eggplant curry good for blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with ICMR-NIN data for cooked spiced brinjal preparations and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central for eggplant cooked. No exact dish-level entry exists in any approved primary source for 'Baingan Patiyala' as a named preparation; values are adapted estimates from the closest approved-source analogues. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (cooked eggplant, GI ~15). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this specific stir-fried spiced brinjal preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level as of 2024. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported at the composite dish level in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this preparation as of 2024. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation; consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA data for plant-based stir-fried dishes). 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — entirely plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients). 5. potassium_mg (285 mg/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central values for cooked eggplant (~123 mg/100 g), tomato (~237 mg/100 g), and onion (~146 mg/100 g) weighted by approximate ingredient proportions; this value should be confirmed with direct analysis. 6. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient source as it is not on the approved source list; ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central were used instead. 7. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 A00GJ are approximate matches only; no exact dish-level ontology code exists for Baingan Patiyala.
